reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy of church/foundation maintainers; pluralism of non-state school networks; protection of differentiated educational choice within the maintained sector; commitment to equity where compatible with maintainer independence
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers' institutional autonomy, admission discretion, and network sustainability
reactions.public_good_frame: Pluralism serves the public good when non-state maintainers operate under transparent, proportionate rules that do not subordinate their educational mission to state-designed SES-proxy enforcement; equity gains are legitimate only if they preserve maintainer discretion over their own admission philosophy
reactions.argument: S1 is acceptable in principle if (a) reserved-seat quotas apply only to state-maintained schools, not church/foundation providers, preserving our admission autonomy; (b) if quotas do extend to non-state schools, they are negotiated as voluntary guidance with multi-year phase-in, not compliance benchmarks; (c) the SES-proxy instrument is demonstrably low-gaming and auditable, not a bureaucratic black box. The scenario's own evidence acknowledges that Hungary lacks an administrative student-level SES register and any proxy is gameable. We cannot align our admission rules with a contested, ungamed instrument. Separately, we note that the scenario assumes 'non-state maintainers will apply comparable admission rules rather than absorbing displaced selective demand' — this is not an assumption we accept. Our schools serve educational missions (religious formation, pedagogical philosophy) distinct from state provision; admission rules must reflect those missions, not state equity targets. If the state redesigns selective admission to reduce SES skew, that is a legitimate state choice for state schools, but it does not bind us. The outreach and assessment redesign investments benefit state schools; we do not oppose them, but we reserve the right to maintain our own admission criteria if they are transparent and non-discriminatory under applicable law.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support a modified S1 if: (1) reserved-seat rules apply only to state-maintained schools; (2) if negotiated extension to non-state schools occurs, it is framed as voluntary sectoral alignment with a multi-year phase-in and explicit carve-outs for schools with explicitly stated religious/pedagogical admission criteria; (3) the SES-proxy is piloted and audited for gaming before any quota enforcement; (4) the scenario acknowledges that non-state demand absorption is a maintainer choice, not an assumption. We would oppose S1 if it attempts to impose SES-proxy quotas on church/foundation schools as a compliance benchmark without negotiation.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy and legal right of church/foundation maintainers to operate selective schools; protection of educational diversity; concern that state-maintained caps do not reduce overall sorting if non-state maintainers fill the gap, and that such caps constrain institutional choice without sectoral parity
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers' legal right to operate selective schools and expand intake without artificial legislative caps
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational pluralism serves the public good when non-state schools retain operational autonomy to serve families who value their pedagogical/religious mission; equity is not advanced if state caps on selective places simply shift selective demand to non-state schools, leaving overall sorting unchanged but restricting institutional choice
reactions.argument: S2 is internally contradictory in a way that directly threatens our institutional interests. The scenario's own assumptions and uncertainties openly state: 'Caps on state-maintained schools are not offset by non-state maintainers expanding selective intake [assumption — directly contradicted by this scenario's own political risks and uncertainties]'. The scenario itself flags that non-state expansion defeats the cap. Worse, the mechanism assumes 'Ministry + maintainers — negotiate cap coverage for church/foundation providers to prevent offset' — this is an attempt to bind non-state schools to legislative caps negotiated for the state sector. We do not accept this. If the state chooses to cap and shrink selective intake in state-maintained schools, that is the state's sovereign choice. But a negotiated 'cap coverage' that extends limits to church/foundation providers violates institutional autonomy: it subordinates our schools to a state-sector equity logic that may not align with our mission or our families' educational preferences. The scenario also notes the mechanical risk: 'as caps tighten, residual selective places become even more SES-skewed absent an S1-type admission-rule change' — so S2 without S1 makes state selective places scarcer AND more skewed, perversely worsening the equity outcome the scenario claims to pursue. We conclude: if the state shrinks selective intake via caps, demand shifts to non-state schools, sorting is not reduced, and our institutional freedom is constrained without a coherent public good. We oppose S2 unless the state exempts non-state providers from cap coverage and funds S1-type admission-rule reform for state schools simultaneously.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would conditionally support S2 if: (1) caps apply only to state-maintained schools; (2) non-state maintainers are explicitly excluded from 'cap coverage' negotiations; (3) S1-type admission-rule reform is simultaneously implemented for state selective schools so that residual places are less skewed; (4) the state acknowledges that demand absorption by non-state schools is not a defeat of the reform but a legitimate function of educational pluralism. We would oppose S2 if it attempts to extend caps to non-state schools, either as negotiated 'coverage' or later through legislative amendment.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy of church/foundation maintainers; the right to offer differentiated (including selective/academic-track) education aligned with institutional mission; concern that comprehensive-school mandates subordinate pedagogical diversity to a single state-preferred model; legal and constitutional protections for non-state education under Hungarian and EU law
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers' legal right and operational capacity to offer selective, academically differentiated education as part of their institutional mission
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational diversity and institutional pluralism serve the public good; a single comprehensive-school model imposed across all maintainers (state and non-state) reduces pedagogical choice and constrains families' ability to select education aligned with their values; equity within a diverse system (where some schools are selective/academic and others are comprehensive) is legitimate if access rules are transparent and non-discriminatory
reactions.argument: S3 is the most direct institutional threat of the four scenarios because it proposes to abolish the 6/8-year selective entry structure entirely and impose a common comprehensive lower-secondary phase. Critically, the implementation step states: 'Ministry + maintainers — extend the common-phase mandate to church/foundation schools to limit flight'. This is not a negotiation; it is a mandate. We reject this. The scenario's own equity_impact section acknowledges the central risk: 'middle-class and church/foundation exit to non-state or private schools. That exit does not harm the exiting pupils, but it can relocate rather than remove sorting.' The scenario names church/foundation flight as the mechanism that could defeat the reform — yet proposes to mandate that we participate anyway. This is a logical and institutional contradiction: if the reform's integrity depends on preventing our exit, the state is attempting to bind our institutional mission to a particular equity outcome, subordinating our pedagogical autonomy to a state equity logic. Under Hungarian education law (Nkt.), non-state maintainers have the legal right to set their own admission and curriculum within national standards. S3 as described would violate that right by legislating away the structural choice (6/8-year selective entry) that many of our schools offer. Second, the scenario's own evidence base is contested on the mechanism most important to us: the peer-effect channel is tagged weak/contested, and the scenario notes that 'the aggregate equity link rests on it'. If the equity gain depends on a contested mechanism, and the only way to prevent our exit is to mandate our participation, the reform is not justified by evidence — it is an ideological imposition disguised as equity policy. Third, the scenario assumes teacher supply can be stabilized, yet implementation_planning flags a strong teacher shortage. Our schools often employ teachers trained in subject specialization (e.g., for academic tracks); a mandate to staff heterogeneous comprehensive classes without a demonstrated teacher supply is an unfunded mandate. We oppose S3 unambiguously.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would conditionally accept a modified S3 if: (1) the common-phase mandate applies only to state-maintained schools; (2) church/foundation schools retain the right to offer selective 6/8-year entry if they wish, subject to transparent, non-discriminatory admission rules; (3) if we choose to participate in a comprehensive model, it is a voluntary alignment with multi-year notice and no penalty for non-participation; (4) teacher supply for differentiated instruction is demonstrated, funded, and available; (5) the equity mechanism (peer effects) is validated before rollout. We would oppose S3 as described because it subordinates our institutional mission to a state equity mandate and violates our legal autonomy under the Nkt.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy of church/foundation maintainers; preservation of selective/differentiated education as a valid institutional choice; concern that non-structural, targeted-investment approaches respect both equity goals and educational pluralism; commitment to equity outcomes where they do not require subordinating institutional mission
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers' interest in retaining selective education structures and admission autonomy while supporting equitable outcomes through targeted investment in disadvantaged schools
reactions.public_good_frame: Equity and pluralism are compatible if non-state schools retain discretion over their educational model while the state invests robustly in disadvantaged schools. Targeted investment respects institutional autonomy (non-state schools are not mandated to change structure) and addresses equity at its root (resource and support gaps in disadvantaged schools) rather than through structural mandates that restrict educational choice
reactions.argument: S4 is the only scenario that is compatible with institutional autonomy and educational pluralism as core public goods. It retains the 6/8-year gimnazium structure unchanged, meaning church/foundation schools that offer selective education face no mandate to abolish or fundamentally alter their model. Instead, the scenario routes intensive support (TEIP-style targeted funding, curriculum standards, reduced retention) to disadvantaged general schools. This respects the pluralist premise: some schools serve academic/selective missions, others serve comprehensive/neighborhood communities, and both are legitimate if resources and support are equitably distributed. The scenario acknowledges a key uncertainty: 'Can downstream compensation offset, not merely mask, early sorting at ages 10-12?' We agree this is contested. But S4 does not assume compensation alone will solve everything; it is honest about the limits. Critically for us, S4 does not attempt to extend equity rules to non-state schools; it invests in the state sector to improve outcomes for disadvantaged pupils. If a low-SES pupil attends a disadvantaged general school, S4 ensures that school receives intensive support, allowing that pupil to thrive even without early selection into a selective track. A high-ability low-SES pupil still has access to selective schools (via S1-type admission-rule reform in those schools) if families prefer, or can receive differentiated support within their general school via S4 resources. The scenario's own political assessment is pragmatic: S4 is 'less structurally disruptive' than S2/S3, though it notes budget vulnerability (targeted funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation). We accept that risk as preferable to institutional mandates. We support S4 unambiguously, with one condition.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would maintain our support for S4 conditional on: (1) the scenario does not evolve toward imposing admission-rule or structural mandates on non-state schools; (2) targeted funding to disadvantaged schools is genuinely multi-year and protected from first-line fiscal cuts through explicit legislative commitment; (3) the state monitors and publishes comparison-group results on whether compensation reduces, not merely masks, the equity gradient; (4) if results show that compensation alone is insufficient, S4 does not morph into S1/S2/S3-style structural mandates on non-state schools, but instead funds additional support (S1-type rule reform in state schools, further targeted investment, expanded diagnostic services). We would withdraw support if the state uses S4 as a temporary placeholder, later legislating structural mandates on non-state schools when political conditions allow.
